meta.title: Atomic Design: From Theory to Production
meta.excerpt: How we structured our component library using atomic design principles for maximum reusability

props.body: Atomic Design is a methodology for creating design systems with five distinct levels: Atoms, Molecules, Organisms, Templates, and Pages. We adapted it for our React component library, focusing on the first three levels for maximum reusability.

props.text: Coined by Brad Frost, Atomic Design breaks UI into fundamental building blocks that compose into increasingly complex structures. Think of it like chemistry: atoms combine to form molecules, which combine to form organisms.
Atoms are the smallest, most fundamental components. They cannot be broken down further while still being functional. Each atom handles one specific UI responsibility.

features.title: FormInput
features.description: Text input with label, error handling, and validation states. Used in 15+ locations.

features.title: FormSelect
features.description: Dropdown selection with shadcn/ui styling and accessibility support.

features.title: FormCheckbox
features.description: Checkbox with label and error states for boolean inputs.

features.title: DatePicker
features.description: Calendar-based date selection with validation and format handling.

props.text: Molecules combine atoms into functional units. They handle specific UI patterns that appear repeatedly across the application.
Organisms are complete, standalone features. They combine molecules and atoms into fully functional UI sections. In our case, each form step is an organism.

props.title: Shared Step Pattern
props.body: We identified steps that appear in multiple forms (Personal Info, Property Info, Schedule) and made them accept a generic store hook. This allows the same organism to work with different Zustand stores.

features.title: Maximum Reusability
features.description: FormInput is used in 15+ locations. One change updates all instances automatically.

features.title: Consistent Quality
features.description: All inputs share the same validation patterns, error handling, and accessibility features.

features.title: Independent Testing
features.description: Each atom, molecule, and organism can be unit tested in isolation.

features.title: Team Scalability
features.description: New developers understand the structure immediately. Clear boundaries reduce conflicts.

idealFor: Building design systems or component libraries
Multiple teams working on the same codebase
Products with multiple similar forms or flows
notIdealFor: Rapid prototypes that will be discarded
Projects with highly unique UI per page

props.text: Here is how our atomic architecture handles the multi-step form system with three different form types sharing components:

props.code:
// Three forms, sharing 60% of their components

// Service Request Form (5 steps)
<ServiceFormContainer>
  <PersonalInfoStep store={useFormStore} />    // Shared
  <ServiceDetailsStep />                        // Unique
  <PropertyInfoStep store={useFormStore} />    // Shared
  <ScheduleStep store={useFormStore} />        // Shared
  <ReviewStep />                               // Unique
</ServiceFormContainer>

// Contact Form (5 steps)
<ContactFormContainer>
  <PersonalInfoStep store={useContactStore} /> // Shared
  <InquiryTypeStep />                          // Unique
  <PropertyInfoStep store={useContactStore} /> // Shared
  <ScheduleStep store={useContactStore} />     // Shared
  <ReviewStep />                               // Unique
</ContactFormContainer>

// Result: 3 forms, 45 total components, 40% code reuse

decisions.result: Use responsive design in existing component

props.body: Start with atoms, compose upward. Identify shared patterns early and extract them. The initial investment in structure pays dividends as your application grows. Our 52-component system handles 3 different form flows with 40% code reuse - that is the power of atomic design.
